/* Planned economy */ Hayek being used to make this claim in wiki-voice is very nearly [[WP:PROFRINGE]] it's so bad. It is, at the very least, a serious violation of [[WP:NPOV]] and I would argue that Hayek's opinions on socialism are [[WP:UNDUE]] inclusion as he was not a thorough scholar on this topic but rather a naked ideologue.

The Soviet Union was not "nominally" a socialist state. It was, in fact, a socialist state according to the opening paragraph of this article.

The difference between "democratic socialism" and "social democracy" is the difference between midnight and 10:00 AM. Democratic socialism is pure socialism that was achieved through democratic election, rather than revolution; social democracy is a hybrid between socialism and capitalism, leaning heavily toward the latter.
Trying to make this easier to understand.

Restored revision 1106791517 by [[Special:Contributions/Czello|Czello]] ([[User talk:Czello|talk]]): Same as before, stop removing sourced content

Left wing and far left are a point of view from a political standpoint that have no weight on the social welfare in mixed economies in a world where the concepts of right and left are archaic compared to globalism vs nationalism especially when we have libertarian socialists (assuming liberalism is the "right", where does put it on the left-right axis?) Definitions should be neutral, maybe put "considered left" later on in the corpus not at the beginning, else it reflects a bias.

Some authoritative sources refer to conservatism when introducing 'radical right'. So should conservativeism be viewed as far right? In Europe, social liberals and moderate social democrats also call themselves socialists. Socialism in Europe includes many things called trade liberalism in the United States.
Swapping out the bottom right image with a better example, as nobody in Australia describes the Australian Labor Party as even remotely socialist.
